location.office_time_required: willingness to travel roughly once a month for team or customer meetings

secondary_archetype: Generalist PM with developer-tools / B2B SaaS focus

level_signals: Full end-to-end ownership of product areas
Autonomy described as 'rare at most companies'
Expected to contribute to broader product strategy and roadmap as company scales
0-to-1 experience and independent prioritization calls expected

must_haves.raw_text_from_jd: B2B SaaS experience — you understand the developer or enterprise buyer and have shipped products for them
One of: deep domain knowledge in auth, identity, or developer tooling, or a strong track record building developer-facing products specifically
Real 0-to-1 experience — you've started something from scratch, not just inherited a roadmap
Customer obsession in practice, not just on a resume — you talk to users constantly and it visibly shapes what you build
Bias to action — you move fast, make decisions without perfect information, and iterate rather than waiting for consensus
Strong cross-functional collaboration — you've worked closely with design, engineering, and marketing, and you know how to bring those relationships to get the best work out of a team
Based in the United States — SF or NYC is a plus but not required
Willingness to travel roughly once a month for team or customer meetings

nice_to_haves.raw_text_from_jd: Experience with pricing and packaging decisions — understanding how product and monetization interact
Background at a company with a PLG motion or developer-first go-to-market
Based in the United States — SF or NYC is a plus but not required

scope.surface: Authentication, identity, authorization, and developer-facing enterprise infrastructure products
primary_metrics_inferred: Feature adoption and usage by developer customers
Customer-defined success metrics per shipped product area
core_responsibilities: Own specific product areas with full accountability for outcomes — from discovery through delivery and iteration
Talk to customers regularly and synthesize what you learn into clear, prioritized product direction
Partner tightly with engineering and design to move from concept to shipped product quickly
Define success metrics and use them to evaluate whether what you shipped actually worked
Work cross-functionally with marketing, sales, and GTM to bring new features and products to market effectively
Identify 0-to-1 opportunities — things that don't exist yet but should — and drive them from blank page to launch
Make hard prioritization calls with limited information and defend them clearly
Contribute to the broader product strategy and roadmap as the company scales

green_flags: Transparent about company stage: $100M Series C, $2B valuation with named investors
Notable customer logos: OpenAI, Cursor, Perplexity, Vercel, Plaid — strong signal of product-market fit
Explicit about genuine scope and autonomy being 'rare at most companies'
Fully remote within the US with no hard city requirement
Honest about what the team culture demands (high-caliber engineers with product instincts)
red_flags: No compensation range disclosed despite 'Competitive pay' language
Visa sponsorship status not addressed anywhere in the JD — unclear
Travel expectation (~once a month) is a logistics requirement not immediately obvious from remote-first framing

Domain requirement ('One of: deep domain knowledge in auth, identity, or developer tooling, or a strong track record building developer-facing products specifically') uses 'OR' structure which adds some ambiguity about what's sufficient

fields_inferred_not_stated: seniority.level (inferred from ownership scope, autonomy language, and 0-to-1 expectations; no years stated)
scope.primary_metrics_inferred (inferred from responsibilities; not directly listed in JD)
scope.business_model (inferred from product description and customer base)
location.work_auth (inferred as no-sponsorship from 'Based in the United States' requirement with no mention of sponsorship)
ambiguities: The domain requirement uses an OR clause: 'deep domain knowledge in auth, identity, or developer tooling, OR a strong track record building developer-facing products specifically' — it is unclear whether just developer-facing track record (without auth/identity knowledge) is truly sufficient or a lower bar.

'Based in the United States' is listed under 'Logistics' alongside nice-to-have city preferences (SF/NYC), making it unclear whether US residence is a hard requirement or a strong preference. Treated as hard here.
Visa sponsorship is completely unaddressed. Work auth classified as no-sponsorship based on US-only framing, but this is inferred.
